startScreenOptions.title: AN62.1-AN62.6 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 4 embedded questions for AN62.1-AN62.6.

interactions.label: The tremor in Parkinson's disease worsens during intentional voluntary movement

params.question: The tremor in Parkinson's disease worsens during intentional voluntary movement such as reaching for an object.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: The narration explicitly stated the tremor was 'worst at rest.' The Parkinsonian rest tremor is driven by basal ganglia dysfunction and characteristically diminishes during voluntary movement. A tremor that worsens during intentional movement (intention tremor) points to cerebellar pathology instead.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — this statement is false. The narration described the Parkinsonian tremor as 'worst at rest.' A resting tremor diminishes during voluntary movement, which distinguishes it from the intention tremor of cerebellar disease that worsens as the hand approaches its target.

metadata.title: The tremor in Parkinson's disease worsen

interactions.label: A patient with damage to Broca's area in the left inferior frontal gyrus would m

params.question: A patient with damage to Broca's area in the left inferior frontal gyrus would most likely present with:

answers.text: Fluent but meaningless speech with impaired comprehension

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: This describes Wernicke's aphasia, caused by damage to area 22 in the left superior temporal gyrus. In Wernicke's aphasia, speech output is fluent but lacks meaning, and the patient cannot comprehend spoken language.

Correct! Broca's area (areas 44 and 45) is the motor speech region. Damage causes Broca's aphasia — the patient understands spoken language but cannot produce speech fluently, as the narration stated.

answers.text: Complete loss of vision in the opposite visual field

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Contralateral visual field loss (homonymous hemianopia) results from damage to the primary visual cortex (area 17) along the calcarine sulcus in the occipital lobe, not the frontal lobe.

answers.text: Loss of sensation on the opposite side of the body

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Contralateral sensory loss results from damage to the postcentral gyrus (primary somatosensory cortex, areas 1, 2, 3) in the parietal lobe, not the inferior frontal gyrus.

overallFeedback.feedback: Broca's area (areas 44/45) in the left inferior frontal gyrus controls speech motor programming. Its damage causes non-fluent speech with preserved comprehension. Remember: Broca's = Broken speech; Wernicke's = Wrong words.

Well done! Broca's aphasia is a non-fluent aphasia — the patient knows what they want to say but struggles to produce it. This localises the lesion to areas 44 and 45 in the left inferior frontal gyrus.

interactions.label: In Parkinson's disease, loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra cau

params.question: In Parkinson's disease, loss of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra causes the motor symptoms primarily by:

answers.text: Overactivating the direct pathway, producing excessive involuntary movements

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Direct pathway overactivity produces excessive movement such as chorea, as seen in Huntington's disease. Parkinson's disease features the opposite — reduced movement (bradykinesia) due to indirect pathway dominance.

answers.text: Disrupting the Papez circuit, causing emotional and memory disturbance

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The Papez circuit (hippocampus → fornix → mammillary bodies → anterior thalamus → cingulate gyrus) governs memory and emotion in the limbic system. It is not part of the basal ganglia motor circuit.

answers.text: Shifting the balance toward the indirect pathway, suppressing thalamic drive to the motor cortex

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! Dopamine normally facilitates the direct pathway and restrains the indirect pathway. When dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra degenerate, the inhibitory indirect pathway dominates, suppressing thalamic activation of the motor cortex and producing bradykinesia, rigidity, and resting tremor.

answers.text: Directly damaging the corticospinal tract, causing upper motor neuron weakness

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The corticospinal tract is a cortical projection fibre system passing through the internal capsule. Its damage causes upper motor neuron signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia), which are distinct from the hypokinetic features of Parkinson's disease.

overallFeedback.feedback: The basal ganglia operate through two opposing pathways: the direct (facilitatory) and indirect (inhibitory). Dopamine from the substantia nigra normally keeps these in balance. In Parkinson's disease, dopamine loss lets the indirect pathway dominate, suppressing thalamic output and reducing voluntary movement.

Excellent! The direct pathway facilitates movement and the indirect pathway inhibits it. Dopamine loss tips the balance toward inhibition, explaining why Parkinson's patients are slow, stiff, and tremulous.

metadata.title: In Parkinson's disease, loss of dopamine

interactions.label: Which artery in the Circle of Willis is the most common site for a berry aneurys

params.question: Which artery in the Circle of Willis is the most common site for a berry aneurysm?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! The anterior communicating artery, which bridges the two anterior cerebral arteries across the midline, is the most common site for berry aneurysms. These congenital outpouchings form at arterial bifurcation points where the vessel wall is structurally weakest.
The middle cerebral artery is the largest branch of the internal carotid and the most common territory for ischaemic stroke, but it is not the most common site for berry aneurysms.

The posterior communicating artery is a notable aneurysm site — its rupture classically compresses cranial nerve III, causing a fixed dilated pupil and ptosis. However, it ranks behind the anterior communicating artery in frequency.

The posterior cerebral artery arises from the basilar artery and supplies the occipital lobe, thalamus, and medial temporal lobe. Aneurysms here are considerably less common than at the anterior communicating artery.

overallFeedback.feedback: The narration explicitly identified the anterior communicating artery as the most common site for berry aneurysms. Berry aneurysms are congenital bulges at arterial bifurcation points — and the AComA, connecting the two anterior cerebral arteries, is the most vulnerable junction.

Well done! The anterior communicating artery is the single most common site for intracranial berry aneurysms. Rupture typically causes subarachnoid haemorrhage, presenting with sudden-onset severe headache.
